## Performance Evaluation

This section reports performance metrics and evaluation figures generated from
the latest completed run of the selected experiment. The results are loaded
from an exported thesis result snapshot (with a local tracking-store fallback)
to maintain consistency with recorded evaluation artifacts while improving
render-time reproducibility.

```{python}
#| label: perf_eval_setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from PIL import Image

from thesis_mlflow_results import load_experiment_snapshot, format_key_metrics

EXPERIMENT_NAME = "btc_td3_tradingenv_reduced_features"
snapshot = load_experiment_snapshot(EXPERIMENT_NAME)
finished = snapshot.latest_finished
```

### Selected Metrics from the Latest Completed Run

```{python}
#| echo: false

if finished is None:
    print(f"No finished runs available for experiment: {EXPERIMENT_NAME}")
else:
    key_metrics_df = format_key_metrics(finished.get("evaluation_report"))
    if key_metrics_df.empty:
        print("No evaluation report artifact found for the latest completed run.")
    else:
        display(key_metrics_df)
```

### Evaluation Metadata Snapshot

```{python}
#| echo: false

if finished is not None:
    params = finished.get("params", {})
    latest = finished.get("latest_metrics")
    rows = [
        ("Run Name", finished.get("run_name")),
        ("Status", finished.get("status")),
        ("Start Time", str(finished.get("start_time"))),
        ("End Time", str(finished.get("end_time"))),
        ("Seed", params.get("seed")),
        ("Train Size", params.get("data_train_size")),
        ("Algorithm", params.get("training_algorithm")),
        ("Reward Type", params.get("env_reward_type")),
        ("Training Steps (latest metric)", None if latest is None else latest.get("training_steps")),
        ("Final Reward (latest metric)", None if latest is None else latest.get("final_reward")),
    ]
    meta_df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Field", "Value"])
    display(meta_df)
```

### Evaluation Plots

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

if finished is None:
    print("No finished run available to display evaluation plots.")
else:
    plots = finished.get("evaluation_plots", {})
    ordered = [
        ("Merged comparison", "merged_comparison"),
        ("Actual returns", "actual_returns"),
        ("Rewards", "rewards"),
        ("Positions", "positions"),
    ]

    for title, key in ordered:
        plot_path = plots.get(key)
        if plot_path and Path(plot_path).exists():
            img = Image.open(plot_path)
            fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
            ax.imshow(img)
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(title)
            plt.show()
        else:
            print(f"{title}: artifact not found")
```

### Discussion: Feature Redundancy and State Representation Quality

The empirical results should be interpreted with caution when comparing large and
small feature sets, because reinforcement learning performance is highly
sensitive to the quality of the state representation. In particular, increasing
the number of input variables does not guarantee improved performance. A larger
feature set can increase the dimensionality of the learning problem while adding
little incremental information if many variables encode similar market behavior
(for example, multiple indicators derived from closely related moving-average or
momentum transformations).

This issue is especially important in actor-critic methods. The critic must
learn a stable mapping from state-action pairs to expected returns under noisy
financial dynamics. When the state vector contains redundant or weakly
informative features, the signal-to-noise ratio available to the critic may
deteriorate. In practice, this can manifest as unstable policy updates, noisy
training curves, and weak out-of-sample performance, even when the optimization
loss appears well-behaved.

From a methodological perspective, these observations support a hypothesis-driven
approach to feature engineering rather than a purely additive one. In practical
terms, this means that indiscriminately adding available variables to the model
is unlikely to improve reinforcement learning performance and may instead reduce
it. Features should be selected because they provide complementary information
about market structure, volatility, liquidity, or temporal regime behavior, not
merely because they are available. This is consistent with the role of feature
ablation studies in the present workflow: reduced, non-redundant feature sets
may yield more stable learning and more interpretable behavior than larger but
highly overlapping representations.

More broadly, this result highlights a key difference between statistical
modelling convenience and reinforcement learning utility. In RL, the state
representation affects not only prediction quality, but also credit assignment,
exploration efficiency, and policy optimization stability. Consequently, feature
selection should be treated as a central component of experimental design rather
than a secondary preprocessing detail.
